frameworks.name: cost of self-control
frameworks.one_line: self-control failure reflects cognitive costs outweighing perceived benefits of resisting temptation

frameworks.name: effort discounting
frameworks.one_line: subjective value of options is discounted by the effort required to obtain them

frameworks.name: opportunity cost
frameworks.one_line: pre-commitment strategies framed as economically rational responses to anticipated self-control costs

core_question: Can the subjective cost of exerting self-control be quantified at the within-subject level via revealed willingness-to-pay?
key_assumptions: self-control exertion is an effortful, cost-incurring process
subjective cost is revealed by monetary amounts a person will pay to avoid needing self-control
pre-commitment value reflects anticipated future self-control cost
manipulations: level of temptation in the decision task
key_findings: humans pay to avoid having to exert self-control
willingness-to-pay scales with increasing temptation level
self-control cost is modulated by motivational incentives
self-control cost is modulated by stress exposure
interpretations: a psychophysical economic task can index moment-to-moment self-control costs within-subject
the approach validates cross-disciplinary theory linking effortful control to economic pre-commitment
